Data:
Hi, I just want to share something. I really want to join organizations to help me overcome my social anxiety, but I'm also considering my situation. I live quite a distance from the main campus, and I can say that it's taking a huge toll on me. I have to wake up early just to arrive at school a few minutes before my first class, and after my last class, it takes me at least two hours to get home. I feel more exhausted from the commute than from school itself. 

We’re not that well-off as a family, so I don’t have the privilege of renting a place near the school, which is another reason I'm hesitant about joining organizations. I'm also afraid that it might cost me a lot to join one. I would prefer organizations that mostly have online interactions to avoid affecting my finances and time too much. 

I know I’m not a sympathetic person; I feel numb sometimes, which makes it hard for me to understand other people's situations and emotions. That's why I was considering joining Rotaract and the Red Cross, but based on what I witnessed with my friends in junior high, it seems to involve a lot of costs and requires physical presence.
English_Translation: hi, i just want to share something. i really want to join organizations to help me overcome my social anxiety, but i'm also considering my situation. i live quite a distance from the main campus, and i can say that it's taking a huge toll on me. i have to wake up early just to arrive at school a few minutes before my first class, and after my last class, it takes me at least two hours to get home. i feel more exhausted from the commute than from school itself. we're not that well-off as a family, so i don't have the privilege of renting a place near the school, which is another reason i'm hesitant about joining organizations. i'm also afraid that it might cost me a lot to join one. i would prefer organizations that mostly have online interactions to avoid affecting my finances and time too much. i know i'm not a sympathetic person; i feel numb sometimes, which makes it hard for me to understand other people's situations and emotions. that's why i was considering joining rotaract and the red cross, but based on what i witnessed with my friends in junior high, it seems to involve a lot of costs and requires physical presence.

Data:
Just letting out my frustration about that one organization in TM. We have an upcoming event for World Tourism Day. It was originally scheduled for Friday, but due to the typhoon, it was rescheduled to Monday, September. We were only informed on Tuesday, September, that we would have the event.

We had to prepare activities like the yell, booth making, food preparation, poster making, games, quizzes, etc., which all require a lot of time. On top of that, we had schoolwork and classes, so we couldn’t fully focus on the event. We had no free time and had classes all day, even on Saturday. Keep in mind, we were only informed on Tuesday, and Friday was already scheduled for the event without suspension, despite heavy rains for days. We also had to buy materials for our designated area.

Now we had to ask about our classes if they would push through, and we were told, ‘It’s up to you,’ because they couldn’t request the VPAA to make our classes asynchronous, claiming there was no time. Last week, during the meeting, nobody considered the possibilities or risks that could happen. Why should we have to ask the teachers to request no classes when we didn’t organize the event? If someone gets absent for a class unrelated to TM, it’s our fault. There are unclear instructions and feedback, and responses are very unprofessional. You guys are supposed to be college students. What’s the point of an organization if there’s no proper planning and organization? Do better
English_Translation: just letting out my frustration about that one organization in tm. we have an upcoming event for world tourism day. it was originally scheduled for friday, but due to the typhoon, it was rescheduled to monday, september. we were only informed on tuesday, september, that we would have the event. we had to prepare activities like the yell, booth making, food preparation, poster making, games, quizzes, etc., which all require a lot of time. on top of that, we had schoolwork and classes, so we couldn't fully focus on the event. we had no free time and had classes all day, even on saturday. keep in mind, we were only informed on tuesday, and friday was already scheduled for the event without suspension, despite heavy rains for days. we also had to buy materials for our designated area. now we had to ask about our classes if they would push through, and we were told, 'it's up to you,' because they couldn't request the vpaa to make our classes asynchronous, claiming there was no time. last week, during the meeting, nobody considered the possibilities or risks that could happen. why should we have to ask the teachers to request no classes when we didn't organize the event? if someone gets absent for a class unrelated to tm, it's our fault. there are unclear instructions and feedback, and responses are very unprofessional. you guys are supposed to be college students. what's the point of an organization if there's no proper planning and organization? do better

Data: the psych department is having its first ever pinning ceremony and while this may look like an achievement people should also be aware of how everything was handled behind the scenes first we were only informed about the internship uniforms around july and by july we were already required to have our fitting and payment done because the uniforms needed to be ready for the pinning ceremony did they not consider that not everyone can come up with money that quickly or that not everyone is in cebu to have their fitting right away yes size charts were given but many batchmates said they werent accurate second the initial date set for the pinning ceremony was august a weekday who in their right mind schedules something like this on such short notice and on a weekday not every parent is free to attend and not everyone can easily file a leave of absence if it wasnt brought up during our emergency batch meeting i doubt they would have moved it to a weekend third the restrictions on the uniform were excessive at first we were told to strictly wear only navy blue or black slacks and only blue or dark brown closed shoes at that point they might as well have required a full uniform if students hadnt called them out on it i dont think they would have even adjusted are we just expected to stay quiet and keep pleasing osfa fourth during pinning practices some officers were scolding students for not singing the pinning song properly yet they themselves werent even singing arent they supposed to lead by example one officer even resorted to victim blaming it was announced that uniforms would only be distributed on the afternoon of august with the excuse that many students changed orders and sizes and this was said in a snobbish tone as if it were entirely the students fault but wasnt it the organizations lack of foresight that caused the delays in the first place they only informed us in late july after all i hope this gets posted because people need to know that things are not what they seem they market themselves as the voices of the students but they didnt even ask us if we wanted a pinning ceremony in the first place nor what our thoughts were about the internship uniforms it all comes across as very self serving and entitled this should be a reminder dont join orgs just for something to put on your resume or for clout if youre not truly advocating for the students then who are you really serving

Data: the death certificate of student democracy and why the usc ssc has already flatlined the institutional decay of the usc ssc represents more than mere dysfunction its the systematic evisceration of democratic legitimacy through performative governance masquerading as representation what we are witnessing isnt political theater but a political autopsy on live feed enter these meetings discussions and sessions and youll witness careers being constructed from the wreckage of student concerns while actual problems decompose in purgatory this isnt governance but political masturbation with formal procedure as foreplay voter turnout has plummeted issue resolution has probably collapsed and public trust has evaporated the ssc has devolved into a zero sum battlefield where victory means annihilating your opponent not serving constituents political capital flows exclusively to those who excel at destruction not construction the incentive structure has weaponized toxicity as the primary currency of advancement career parasites are ruthless political opportunists who monetize student suffering for personal advancement they arent public servants they view every campus crisis as campaign gold perfecting the grotesque theater of manufacturing outrage over systemic issues while benefiting from the inequalities they publicly condemn every institutional failure becomes ammunition for their portfolios every administrative breakdown transforms into networking currency at gatherings they dont solve problems but harvest dysfunction for resume material transforming the ssc into their personal audition stage where genuine institutional breakdown becomes raw material for ascension to power their counterparts have devolved into ideological extremists masquerading as principled leaders so intoxicated by their own righteousness theyve forgotten governance requires actual results not moral grandstanding and visions that would never work these zealots would rather watch the student body suffer than compromise a single talking point transforming legitimate positions into religious doctrine where any deviation marks you as a heretic weaponizing paralysis turning governance into performative martyrdom where being spectacularly wrong with conviction trumps being practically right through collaboration the council is a culture of manufactured crisis management where representatives actively seek controversy to boost their profiles they arent solving problems but farming engagement like social media influencers in suits an institution designed to amplify student voices has become the primary mechanism for their silencing we witness here democracys panopticon in miniature wake up carolinians your so called representatives are selling you out for linkedin recommendations the council has been captured by neoliberal careerists who appropriate social justice language while perpetuating the systems they claim to oppose their activism is aesthetic not institutional change traditional governance principles have been abandoned in favor of radical experimentation that benefits only those leveraging division for personal advancement the sscs embrace of performative politics over practical solutions has created tribal warfare where victory is measured in destroyed reputations not solved problems every semester brings cascading crises yet the councils energy is consumed by jurisdiction disputes and procedural warfare the institutional obituary has been written but not delivered the usc supreme student council cause of death autoimmune disorder the body politic rejected its own representative organs until democratic circulation ceased entirely what dies with the ssc isnt just another campus organization its the last formal mechanism through which students can collectively shape their educational experience when that voice disappears power concentrates in offices where student input becomes optional not essential the final diagnosis ten different analytical frameworks all converge on the same conclusion the sscs collapse isnt a future possibility its a present reality were collectively pretending it hasnt happened yet the question isnt whether it will survive but whether anyone will notice when the last meeting is called to order in an empty room where democracy came to die written by whistleblower tired of your performances

Data: fourth year computer science students of the University of San Carlos were held back from graduating Students were on track for graduation this school year but were held back due to the unapproved petition for thesis in the second semester Thesis is a course taken after completing the thesis paper where the latter involves proposing the paper and the former is for presenting it at conferences and submitting the hardbound copy The course is usually offered in the first semester but may also be offered off semester through a petition requiring a minimum number of students Many students withdrew from taking thesis in the first semester after being advised by their thesis teacher who is also the chair of the department of Computer Information Sciences and Mathematics to drop the course and reenroll next semester if they were not ready Students who were finishing implementations and seeking conferences chose to drop the course to avoid failing The petition to offer thesis was initiated in the student information system and signed by students who had papers accepted at conferences Students were informed that the chances of thesis being offered were slim due to limited faculty and room availability Despite finding faculty willing to handle the course efforts were rejected as it was not the students power to choose The department gave new conditions including adviser certificate or conference acceptance Students were later notified that only those who had presented published and were ready for hardbound could take the course reducing eligible students to a few Attempts to communicate with the chair were unproductive and escalations to the dean and vice president of academic affairs were redirected back to the chair Students obtained legal consultation but did not send it to hold a meeting with the administration Repercussions include delayed graduation added financial burden risk of losing latin honors and job offers Many students remain apprehensive about speaking up and as of writing no clarification has been provided by the department
English_Translation: fourth year computer science students of the university of san carlos were held back from graduating students were on track for graduation this school year but were held back due to the unapproved petition for thesis in the second semester thesis is a course taken after completing the thesis paper where the latter involves proposing the paper and the former is for presenting it at conferences and submitting the hardbound copy the course is usually offered in the first semester but may also be offered off semester through a petition requiring a minimum number of students many students withdrew from taking thesis in the first semester after being advised by their thesis teacher who is also the chair of the department of computer information sciences and mathematics to drop the course and reenroll next semester if they were not ready students who were finishing implementations and seeking conferences chose to drop the course to avoid failing the petition to offer thesis was initiated in the student information system and signed by students who had papers accepted at conferences students were informed that the chances of thesis being offered were slim due to limited faculty and room availability despite finding faculty willing to handle the course efforts were rejected as it was not the students power to choose the department gave new conditions including adviser certificate or conference acceptance students were later notified that only those who had presented published and were ready for hardbound could take the course reducing eligible students to a few attempts to communicate with the chair were unproductive and escalations to the dean and vice president of academic affairs were redirected back to the chair students obtained legal consultation but did not send it to hold a meeting with the administration repercussions include delayed graduation added financial burden risk of losing latin honors and job offers many students remain apprehensive about speaking up and as of writing no clarification has been provided by the department

Data: hello im also from manila who transferred to usc last year for my studies although i have a bit of knowledge about the language before i came here or so i thought inside the university you can just tell the professors beforehand that you cant comprehend the language so they can adjust to you but most of the lectures are taught in english anyways cause they might also have foreign students but from timetotime they will speak bisaya here and there with finding friends i think you need to really come out of your shell and go around the class just use english i also noticed that cebuanos are good with english but i think your biggest problem will be communicating outside of the school i noticed that when i use tagalog to ask cebuanos they are more reluctant to answer me back or they would just flat out ignore me i remember one time when i asked a jeepney driver if his route was going to be able to go to the place where i was going at the side of the road before going in the driver sped off without even letting me finish my sentence so i was really hurt that time i also witnessed this kinda attitude around the city i once saw a girl asking around in tagalog and people would just stare at her or walk pass by her i know it might seem discouraging but im just telling my experience to prepare you to the possibilities because your life wont just run inside the school based on my friends they dont like speaking in tagalog cause it is hard for them so my advice to you is to really learn the language i can honestly see the difference from when i was using englishtagalog to communicate to my friends and classmates to eventually speaking the language people will be more willing to entertain and you will really get to know your friends and other people around you start from learning the basics practice it with your friends i know it will be hard to learn another language but dont be shy to practice it with others because thats how youre gonna be able to learn basically what im saying is kapalan mo lang yung mukha mo saka wag mong masyadong damdamin if ever na mangyari sayo yung mga na experience ko goodluck and welcome to usc fellow future carolinian

foreign students but from timetotime they will speak bisaya here and there with finding friends i think you need to really come out of your shell and go around the class just use english i also people around you start from learning the basics practice it with your friends i know it will be hard to learn another language but dont be shy to practice it with others because thats how youre gonna Data: hi i remember asking the same question here in this subreddit when i was about to enter shs too hehehe now im in college time flies anyway i cant really speak jud for cdu because i ended up choosing usc when you enter th grade theyre going to separate you depending on the type of degree program you choose so prepare to say goodbye to your th grade friends if ever you are not classmates with them so for example in your case since youre planning to take up medtech then you will have to choose the s or science blocks those who are planning to take up engineering architecture etc will take up the e or engineering blocks it computer science etc will take up t or technology then apparently pilots math majors etc usually took up the m or math blocks then youre going to have an elective that may be preparing you for your undergraduate program in the e blocks we had an engineering math elective youre going to have a research subject also and from what i know from my friends who took up the s blocks their research teacher required that their research had to be related to medicine and i remember seeing my friends stressed because they really had to investigate and do experiments and stuff meanwhile us under the e blocks usually had to research and read paperworks only and or do interviews because we dont have to do scientific experiments

Data: as a psychology student i will talk about what usc psychology is like yes it is true that usc can produce board topnotchers but before deciding on a school you really need to think about which field of psychology you are inclined to at usc the program is currently more inclined toward industrial or organizational psychology especially hr related fields this is because the program has a strong foundation in io subjects plus courses like group process and training and development which align well with that track although those two subjects can also be useful if you want to become a facilitator during my internship i also heard feedback that some companies see being from usc as a plus usc psychology is also solid when it comes to guidance and counseling since we also have an internship for that area however what usc is not very focused on is the clinical psychology side although the core subjects are there we do not have a clinical psychology internship yet which is honestly a missed opportunity especially for those who want to pursue clinical psychology
English_Translation: as a psychology student i will talk about what usc psychology is like yes it is true that usc can produce board topnotchers but before deciding on a school you really need to think about which field of psychology you are inclined to at usc the program is currently more inclined toward industrial or organizational psychology especially hr related fields this is because the program has a strong foundation in io subjects plus courses like group process and training and development which align well with that track although those two subjects can also be useful if you want to become a facilitator during my internship i also heard feedback that some companies see being from usc as a plus usc psychology is also solid when it comes to guidance and counseling since we also have an internship for that area however what usc is not very focused on is the clinical psychology side although the core subjects are there we do not have a clinical psychology internship yet which is honestly a missed opportunity especially for those who want to pursue clinical psychology

Data: i will be sharing my experience during my undergrad pharmacy student days at usc one might argue that usc is one of the best pharmacy school in this country and pharmacy itself is a difficult program as it is a premed course similar to other premed courses many units per semester the curriculum that was implemented during my time of study had like units per semester so every week the motivation was just surviving to reach the next week by chasing deadlines doing all nighters to study drawing leaves for botany class and being stressed and overwhelmed all at once it was a very vicious cycle me and my batchmates were all on survival mode until each semester ends the amount of information you need to absorb at a short amount of time plus the projects and assignments with deadlines fast approaching is enough to make anyone breakdown and be burned out normal rani all i can say is it is not for the faint of heart
English_Translation: i will be sharing my experience during my undergrad pharmacy student days at usc one might argue that usc is one of the best pharmacy school in this country and pharmacy itself is a difficult program as it is a premed course similar to other premed courses many units per semester the curriculum that was implemented during my time of study had like units per semester so every week the motivation was just surviving to reach the next week by chasing deadlines doing all nighters to study drawing leaves for botany class and being stressed and overwhelmed all at once it was a very vicious cycle me and my batchmates were all on survival mode until each semester ends the amount of information you need to absorb at a short amount of time plus the projects and assignments with deadlines fast approaching is enough to make anyone breakdown and be burned out normal rani all i can say is it is not for the faint of heart

Data: to silliman alumni did the name silliman university help or hurt your job applications be honest hi everyone im currently a rd year engineering student here in silliman university im originally from cebu and to be honest i ended up in silliman because i didnt pass my dream school university of san carlos i love the campus life here good friends peaceful environment less traffic and laid back city but lately ive been overthinking and wondering about the what ifs like would it be easier for me to land a job if i had usc on my resume instead in cebu usc is considered just one step below the big manila schools ateneo la salle up ust and i always assumed that would give graduates an edge what scares me the most is my future after graduation ive heard stories from alumni who applied in manila and they said hr officers there dont even recognize silliman university or dont seem to value it as much as the top schools that really freaked me out so im asking to all the silliman alumni out therehow did the name silliman university affect your job search and applications especially if you applied outside of dumaguete or the visayas id appreciate honest and detailed answers thank you

Data: Hello. i am mechanical engineering undergraduate going into nd year. as i just finished my freshman year, take this reply as a grain of salt; I may not be able to provide the proper information you need. first off, someone's topping the licensure exams does not necessarily mean that the program is naturally better for all. its a sign thatyesits not bad, but nevertheless its individual effort. whether this environment is beneficial to you, you'll have to figure that out yourself. on the same topic, usc actually had a better passing rate overall for first timers than uc.. on the same exam that the uc top-notchers took cit got. btw. granted, i think this is an insignificant difference. secondly, this is what I've heard from my friend studying in cit-u, and my classmates; that usc has a different focus compared to cit-u and possibly uc? I don't have friends from UC. apparently, usc focuses more on automotive, where in higher years we have go-cart related projects. on the other hand, cit-u focuses more on computational and mechatronics. furthermore, usc grads tend to become managers? can't confirm thirdly, if you care about accreditations, uscs bsme is the only university in vis-min that is a ched center of excellence. most importantly, it is a signatory of the washington accord, which promises a streamlined process when working abroad. finally, and most crucially for me, is that usc is a university, which means that there are other programs other than bsme. that is obvious; however, what is usually looked over is the quality of professors across general electives and engineering-related courses. we have physicists handling engineering physics, phds handling uts and ethics, economists handling tcw, and much more. it may not matter to you, but for me it did. bsme is a four year journey, as much as i want to graduate and become a mechanical engineer, i also want to become an engineer that is well-rounded. what may actually keep you sane are the minor subjects or make you insane. plus, you get to mingle with other students from different programs that are at the top of their own games. I have done personal projects with students from the physics, computer science, computer engineering departments. you can do this in other unis but usc has a much more extroverted environment. a lot of students are welcoming, and this is noticeable in how friendly everyone generally is. whether you care about this, its completely up to you. after all, years is a blink in our lifespan but years are full of memories, nevertheless.

Data: though the quality of the profs. well-known psychologists in Cebu usually teach at USC. sir glenn palang daan our dean is the president of psychological association of the philippines. though idk if the same profs were in the undergrad run yesterday. Our undergrad profs who are our idols for their wisdom and teaching seem to have resigned. but currently, our masters professors are good. You will appreciate their teaching even more because they equipped us before in undergrad. my classmates who are not in USC undergrad, make a little extra effort to catch up on what they missed out on. The standards are high and it can be difficult at times, but expect that if you really want good quality of education. I agree with the lack of exposure. I would have benefited more if I had tried a clinical setting in the internship. they focus more on the industrial setting in the guidance. maybe only a minority of graduates will proceed to become psychologists. yes, there are also other profs who are inconsistent and it's a pity that they should impart knowledge to many. There must be some kind of person who is bright but doesn't know how to teach hahaha fortunately, they are few in the faculty. it's not just the psych department that chooses their instructors. even topnotchers on the board.

I went to the office of my program's department after the interviewer the chair of the department recommended me for the merit scholarship. while i was there, the department directed me to the scholarship office and i was told to comply with all my requirements, as well as additional ones. however, I was told that only students whose permanent residences are within Cebu City are considered for the full tuition-fee merit scholarship. I don't live in Cebu City, so I was disheartened at first when they said that. but, the scholarship office said that if and only if I qualify for other scholarships, like ched or dost, it is possible that they shoulder all the other school fees not covered by the k annual designation for tuition and other fees provided by both scholarships. I already prepared all the additional requirements they gave. they gave me the usc scholarship office's contact information so that i may tell them whether or not i have qualified for dost or ched. well, just yesterday i saw my name on the list of passers for dost, so i have contacted them about my award of scholarship. I am still yet to get a reply from them.

Data: hello, afaik in the st and nd years, the subjects are the same in bsa, bsma, and bsia. after second year, you have the option to take the program advancement readiness test part, and if you pass it, you can shift retain to bsa. if not, then either you'll have to shift retain to either bsma or bsia. what sets apart bsma's curriculum compared to other bsma program offerings in other universities is that during your fourth year, you will have your review for certified management accountant cma. whether you want to be a cma or not, this is mandatory as it is integrated in the curriculum itself. it's an international certification and perfect if you are planning to migrate and work abroad. in terms of profs, in the department some are actually good, while some are clearly forced to handle the subject because there are not enough units in the load. some are purely exam, others are purely reporting so just be lucky and choose a schedule. overall i recommend bsma in usc bsma senior
English_Translation: hello, afaik in the st and nd years, the subjects are the same in bsa, bsma, and bsia. after second year, you have the option to take the program advancement readiness test part, and if you pass it, you can shift retain to bsa. if not, then either you'll have to shift retain to either bsma or bsia. what sets apart bsma's curriculum compared to other bsma program offerings in other universities is that during your fourth year, you will have your review for certified management accountant cma. whether you want to be a cma or not, this is mandatory as it is integrated in the curriculum itself. it's an international certification and perfect if you are planning to migrate and work abroad. in terms of professors, in the department some are actually good, while some are clearly forced to handle the subject because there are not enough units in the load. some are purely exam, others are purely reporting so just be lucky and choose a schedule. overall i recommend bsma in usc bsma senior

Data: curriculum - im not entirely familiar with it but i hear its good. its rare to see a uni here in cebu offer marine biology as a program. you can probably ask the bio department for their prospectus list of subjects for the entire curriculum. if you are interested in pursuing further studies such as a masters, etc, its definitely possible especially if you perform well academically. as for the subjects, there will be a lot of subjects you have to take that are not really related to your program but every college student in the philippines has to go through, like communication, arts, pe, etc. it often gets hectic during the semester because of the busy schedule and workload especially since usc tends to put a lot of subjects per semester. by the th year you will accomplish your undergraduate thesis, usually with a group of other people. early morning classes can also go as early as: am. usc is a catholic school as well so there will be required subjects on religion and philosophy. you can join orgs since there are a lot, and you can make friends there as well. extracurriculars can make your campus life quite fun campus - its the biggest campus in cebu and is really quite nice. there is a big library, stadium, etc. and its on a mountainside so a lot of nature and a lot of hiking. it can get quite hot especially in the uniform. however as the other comments said there will definitely be traffic so plan where you stay accordingly. there are many places to eat both inside and outside and the jeepneys that pass go to some malls where you can go to buy stuff.

Data: hi! someone else had the same concern as you on here and this was my reply in case it helps you sad hi! i cant give recommendations regarding usjr bsa being better but i can say that your initial course placement in usc does not matter. the only actual aspect would be a sort of separation from your peers where bsmabsia may be looked down on but from my experience this is very rare and it does not matter at all since all major subjects for the first years of all accounting courses in usc are the same and all students under accounting courses get blocked together after the st sem. you can shift after st sem but it isnt suggested as there is no point to it, the shifting of courses usually happen during part after your second year. to qualify for bsa you must have all major subjects above. cut off you may take repeat classes if you dont pass cut-off and pass part. dont feel too bad about not initially qualifying for bsa as there are so many factors in actually passing the course and the entrance exam is not really one of those factors the department just feels like separating the students. hope this helps!

Data: hello, im a rd year bcsc student. the issues only pertain to this years graduating batch mainly because of the chair but if your asking content wise, its good imo though take this with a grain of salt because I have no real reference in comparison to other schools. - bscs curriculum shifted from years to the years track, and you are not allowed to go for the year version. that is to say that the current year track takers today will be the last - if i could go back, i would rather go cit-u cs, or come usc. i say come because i just see how active their departments are, and their equivalent org cpec is more active compared to ours here. Data: the issues with dcism faculty aren't going away anytime soon. if you want to be taken advantage of and robbed of your tuition fees, take cs. and, this doesn't only apply to the graduating batch. please read previous posts regarding this issue in this subreddit check my profile for my comments and in cfw to understand the problems with the cs program in usc. even my friends who are part of the pioneering batch that are taking the three-year program regret their choice. on the other hand, cpe is a much better program, overall, because of the fields you can specialize in. furthermore, the maths in engineering are much useful than what people demonize them as. the department's council is very active, which makes for a great college experience overall. ironically, did you know that cpe students steamrolled up cebu's komsai week computer science week? yes, they bagged the championship of all the categories from the competitions hosted up.

Data: leadership: our current cs chairperson here in usc has an undergraduate degree bsit in sti college ormoc, msit in usjr, phd in technology management in cebu technological university. while cs chairperson of cit-u took her undergraduate degree in computer science at up-los banos, mscs also at up-los banos, and a doctor of it in cit. between the two, we know that the chairwoman of cit-u is above par compared to that of usc. since cit-u's chairperson is an "isko" through and through. research: no usc's computer science department is not research oriented, the faculty do not have an original research of their own, most of our faculty's names are published in conference proceedings because they act as our advisers, we call them "grammar bots", because their major contributions to our paper are just spelling checks, grammar checks, and punctuation marks. we are forced to put their names because we fear their retaliation, the same thing as what they did to our seniors they did not graduate due to some thesis related issues. there are rumors that our advisers receive financial incentives for every publication with their names on it, when in fact they are just piggybacking with our thesis topics. our department requires us to present our papers in a conference and have it published in a conference proceedings, we do the research, we pay the conference fees while our advisers get the incentives, which is very unfair. advice: if you want a school that is brag worthy then i highly suggest you go to up cebu. the up system has the prestige, recognition, and perceived academic clout. if i had the chance, i could've taken upcat and enrolled in up's cs. if you want employability then you go to uc their main focus is on industry transferable skills. they make sure that their students are employable and work ready. cit-u cs is beefing up their curriculum, simply because the cit-u cs chairwoman is bringing in the "up" culture into cit-u. but if you want to go for usc, go ahead no one is stopping you. but i suggest you take cpe instead, their facility is way better than cs, and their faculty dynamics is less toxic too. I also suggest that you also check the faculty profile, especially where they got their phd's from. you can tell how smart the faculty are based on their academic qualifications, the baseline should be a phd degree in the up system, much better if they had their phd in a qs or the top ranked university.

Data: i took mine almost a year ago now. I don't know if they are going to change it, but it was basically your regular college entrance exam. if you did well in your undergrad, i think the grad entrance exam becomes a "formality" somewhat. not sure anymore on the order, but i remember taking tests.. iqlogic. math algebra, geometry, arithmetic. English spelling, grammar, reading comprehension. science this is for some programs only. I took the exam with other takers and they did not have this part. essay very specific on the program you applied. this one took the longest. all of them have time-limits, with the essay taking the longest. the english exam was the longest. iirc, the exam started at am and ended at noon. the "hardest" was probably the english exam, mostly because of the length, but also because you have to have near mastery of the english language to score high. the essay tests your theoretical knowledge in the program you're applying for, and also if you're updated on the current trends in research and industry. you're given prompts, and you are to write an essay regarding one of the said prompts. as i said, if you've done well in your undergrad, you probably won't be doing bad on this exam. idk how much weight the results of those exams would have on your admission to the program since different departments are run by different chairpersons with differing priorities. in my case, the exam results were a "formality," what mattered most was the interview with the chairperson which came afterwards. my interview experience was quite casual. the chairperson asked me about my undergrad experience, motivation to get a higher degree, goals during and after graduation, and most importantly my research plans. I emphasize the research plans because a ched memo took effect a year or so ago which mandated that grad students must have been published in a peer-reviewed journal publication before they can apply for thesisdissertation defense. this means that you can't graduate if you are not published. two weeks back, usc had an online orientation which stated that the publication requirement for the grad students must be with a peer-reviewed and indexed journal. I won't get into the technical details on journal indexing, but this means that the students' publications must be of a higher quality. this is because most journals and publications are peer-reviewed, but not all are indexed. typically, the journals that are indexed have more stringent review processes thus somewhat low acceptance rate and have quite expensive publication fees typically ranging from php, to php,. so yeah, i suggest not to worry about the exam that much. instead, focus more on what research you want to do as a grad student. try to find what recent publications and projects the faculty of the program you're applying to have done or are doing, and communicate with them if possible. the interview with the chairperson could also be your introduction to the research done by the department program. in any case, see you around the campus!

Data: hello carolinian pharmacist here. I will be sharing my experience during my undergrad pharmacy student days at usc. one might argue that USC is one of the best pharmacy schools in this country and pharmacy itself is a difficult program as it is a pre-med course. similar to other pre-med courses, many units per semester. the curriculum that was implemented during my time of study had like - units per semester so every week the motivation was just surviving to reach the next week by chasing deadlines, doing all-nighters to study, drawing leaves for botany class?, and being stressed and overwhelmed all at once. it was a very vicious cycle. me and my batchmates were all on survival mode until each semester ends. the amount of information you need to absorb in a short amount of time plus the projects and assignments with deadlines fast approaching is enough to make anyone breakdown and be burned out normally. all i can say is it is not for the faint of heart crying for a few minutes then back again was like a norm. hopefully following the new trimester period alleviated the problems we were facing so that there is more breathing room and less cramped up schedules. for context schedules before were arranged like am to pm straight classes for the entire week other than half day saturday classes. then there will be deadlines for projects with exams and quizzes. during your first year, set your expectations right. this is the time you are adjusting to your new environment. it's a struggle for most students during this phase, but I think the faculty is lenient during this time but don't be complacent. if you are aiming for honors this is the best time to save money and grades because tbh after st year it is all downhill haha. in the second year, it's like I was forgotten about this experience. I will start introducing core subjects during my curriculum like pcog, biochem, pharm chem. all i can say is, what hafen second year why are u crying agen. third year is like the screening period, here are other core subjects like hosp pharm, pcol, manuf, and most importantly your thesis insert. In this period, many are either advised to shift transfer or become irreg or fight with their thesis partner. I consider this the screening period because this is literally the wall that you metaphorically have to climb to become a pharmacist intern. fourth year, congratulations on becoming a pharmacist intern you are one step closer to graduating. The internship is fun, you can learn a lot especially since the selected internship sites are big industry partners getting to know and experience the different fields of pharmacy practice. but the feeling of dread is slightly there due to having to prepare for the board exam but that is another story. the seniors during my time i feel so old were also very approachable and amiable, probably because they also shared the same struggles and sentiments. the facilities, labs, and equipment are complete just that the wifi is either not working or there is no signal since the building is on the mountain. overall, this is a stressful and difficult program, the stories and experiences you might have heard are most likely true. the advice i could give to myself if i were taking bs pharmacy again as a freshmen is to be kind to myself, to celebrate small wins even if it's a surprise that i got a shade in zipgrade when it's still ice cream after the exam!

Data: hi op! cs student here from dcism, directly affected by the controversy. it at usc falls under the same department as cs, so if nothing changes, it and is students will continue to face the same treatment. actually, they already have, just without as much public attention. there has always been an unspoken bias in the dept for favoring cs, but if even cs students are now being treated unfairly, what does that say about how it and is students are handled? many it and is students already feel neglected, receiving far less support from the department. even the faculty can testify so. as for the one-year course, as efficient as a compressed curriculum might seem, the biggest concerns are heavier workloads each term, less time for in-depth learning, and greater difficulty balancing academics with internship opportunities. this is something that students in the new curriculum are already worried about for the upcoming semester. if you're considering it, my it friends strongly recommend looking into other schools like cit, which offer better facilities and support. whether pursuing it at usc is a smart choice depends on how willing you are to navigate the challenges and roadblocks the school may present, sometimes unfairly. so with that, i hope you do take the time to consider your options. good luck op!

Data: hello op! I'm one of the students affected by the graduation issue under USC's CS program, and I totally understand your concern. I commend you for asking these questions before committing to usc. usc does have its strengths, such as a good environment for networking and extracurriculars. while i can no longer trust the usc administration, i genuinely believe in the tenacity and spirit of its students. there are active student organizations to engage with and opportunities to connect with local tech groups. usc also has partnerships with companies in the industry, which can be helpful for internships and job opportunities. as for the program, it's struggling right now. the new curriculum is crammed into years, making it extremely fast-paced, leaving little room for failure, and making it difficult to balance academics, research, and internships. there are faculty shortages, limited classrooms, and administrative decisions that have put students in tough situations. you'll find that most students rely on self-study and external resources rather than what is covered in class. it's difficult to say if things will get better or not in the near future. the current structure on the administrative end is not expected to change anytime soon, so there's no guarantee that things will improve quickly. if you do enroll, be prepared for these challenges and be proactive in handling potential setbacks. and while there is still time before the next academic year starts, it wouldn't hurt to explore other schools and their cs programs as well. but if you do choose usc, be ready to navigate the difficulties and commit to a lot of self-learning at a fast pace. good luck, op!

Data: this is a tricky question, hahahaha! my friends from undergraduate studies became my primary circle of friends in ls so he was easy in that part. but generally, easy to make friends? yes. but easy to trust people? not really. however and sadly, you have to be strategic about your friends. know the friends who you can party with but limit who you can trust because usc laws environment can get competitive and toxic. there are others who will wish for one's downfall if you are doing too well in recitals or exams. just, there are really insecure people. as for me, i did gain a lot of friends! i have fun and all! there are a lot of good people as well as insecure ones. just discern who you can trust. you need a circle who will be there for you when things are not going well bad recites, bad exams, personal problems, the kind of circle who will not judge you, understand where you are coming from, and not talk about what you shared to them. but i think if your friends are mostly working students, they don't have the time to indulge in petty situations. for fresh graduates though, they have this tendency to dwell on rankings because it kinda is the thing they are used to.

English_Translation: hi! as someone who is still confused about what path to take in college, i am considering bs pharma as one of my options, i just wanted to ask if you would recommend taking bs pharma at usc? can you please give me some insights about this course at usc? is it really difficult there at usc or can it be done? hehe thank you so much.:

Data: hello carolinian pharmacist here. it is true that the bs pharmacy is already quite challenging especially if you choose to study in usc because it is a top performing school for pharmacy. during my freshmen years we were about in my batch fast forward to graduation day only of us in my original batch graduated together. the rest either shifted, transferred or had to graduate the next following year. like any other premed courses, there are a lot of units around to units per semester so expect most of your days either studying, complying with your academic workload, or being overwhelmed this is normal. an advantage to studying pharmacy in usc is that it has facilities, connections, accreditations, and prestige. facilities wise, the labs are complete and i heard there are new equipment that recently arrived, there is also an animal house for doing drug studies on rats and mice. connections, usc department of pharmacy have many international university partners, you may get the chance to visit these foreign universities if granted the opportunity. although this is quite competitive because there are limited slots for student selection. aside from this, the internship experience during your th year is also top notch where you get to work at big industry partners hospital, manufacturing, etc. and learn from the best. accreditations, the pharmacy department is a faap paascu level iii accredited program which is the highest accreditation for any pharmacy program offered in the country. I think they are also trying to become a center for development. prestige, there is perceived excellence when you graduate from usc bs pharmacy which translates to respect, and better job opportunities. well that's pretty much the pros about it now onto the cons. less time for yourself, family, and friends. when you take this course in usc, you are going to be very busy with managing your academic workload. i had missed a lot of family gatherings, birthdays, celebrations, and etc. due to being occupied with acads. it is quite a bummer really but that's to be expected given the amount of units per semester which is the same for any premed courses. the building is really far from the portal, if you are running late and there is no shuttle or you don't drive a vehicle, you have to run uphill. as for the question if it's hard, tbh it's hard. There is probably no course that is not difficult, you just need to muster the right amount of time, effort, and motivation to finish the course. hope this helps.

Data: Hello! if you're not aware, there's a big issue regarding how the department is being handled. you can read all about it on the carolinian freedom wall. carolinianfreedomwall carolinianfreedomwall carolinianfreedomwall carolinianfreedomwall tldr; th year students can't graduate for another year because thesis ii is not being offered despite having three professors that have the credentials are willing to teach it. a comment in carolinianfreedomwall should tell you that usc computer science is nothing special: "if your goal is to get your child an education the subjects taught in uc, cit, usjr and uv are just the same as the ones taught in usc. nothing special being taught here to increase your child's employability or net worth." furthermore, a comment in carolinianfreedomwall highlights how research is being handled by the faculty of dcism in general: "a notable difference i have observed is the level of faculty involvement in refining research methodologies. in our department, it often seems that the adviser's role is limited to correcting grammar and spotting typos, rather than actively scrutinizing and strengthening the methodology." and, it's not just about research. despite these, it's not all doom and gloom; however, the issue mentioned above is real. ask yourself, are all the good things in usc computer science worth it when abject disregard of the students is a certain for all who dare to take the program. up-cebu and cit-u are great options you can't go wrong with them. if you really want to study in usc for the environment and you like programming, you can still take computer engineering. if you are into ai or datascience, physics is a must for the analytical skills you'll learn through analysis i, ii, and iii. additionally, they have a course on ai and do data science stuff as their research projects.

Data: as a psych student, i'll talk about how is usc psych. yes, it is true that usc can produce topnotchers in the boards. but before you decide on what school, you need to think about what field in psychology you are inclined with. in usc, currently, they are inclined with industrial psychology, the ones in hr. it is because we have a good foundation of the subjects about io, plus we have a group process and training and development which will also align with it. altho those two subjects can also be used if you want to be a facilitator. also, as i did internship, i heard feedback that some companies, plus points if you are from usc. usc psych is also fine with its guidance and counseling aspect. we have an internship on it as well. the not so focus lng tlaga with usc is the clinical psych side. altho the core subjects are there but we do not have internship for it yet. It's a pity especially if you want to do clinical psychology. other things in usc psych. the teachers are good but there are definitely some missed ones but generally you can learn a lot. for the department, I really hate the slowness of assigning teachers to subjects, a week is like an adjustment period, but there are still weeks to come and I'm not sure who the teacher is, which is like a waste of time lol, I'm shocked it's already pre-midterms

Data: why? you need them to be eligible to apply for graduation. how? you can get them by joining co-curricular orgs, or some extra-curricular orgs offer ces activities. there are two different types of ces points that you must get: department-wide ces points and university-wide ces points. department-wide ces are basically ces points within the department. for example, if your program is computer engineering, you need to volunteer as a tutor or help around the department to get your ces hours up. in my department ba comm-med, they initiate and offer volunteer positions in podcasts, radio stations, and other ces initiatives within the department. however, in case you plan to shift, department-wide ces points are non-transferable, meaning you start from zero once you are enrolled in a new program. university-wide ces points are the activities initiated by usc that is open to all carolinians. fun runs for a cause, donation drives, clean-ups, and the like are just some of the stuff they offer.. additionally, helping in the community visit will guarantee you up to ces points. for your ces points to be properly credited to you, filling out the required reflection forms properly are needed. to track your points, you can ask your department council's ces representative to be updated on how many ces points you've gathered over the course of the school year. if you're within sas, you need a total of department-initiated ces points, and university-initiated ces points. based on what i've checked with soe, you need a total of ces points. of those must come from department-initiated CES activities, with just covering university-initiated activities. please ask your ces representative for valid information!! disclaimer: i do not have all the answers, take all the information with a grain of salt!

Data: hello, personally downtown campus where pol sci is located is not accessible.. most of the entrances have stairs, and they can be steep too. doors are knobs and not all restrooms are large enough for a wheelchair. tc can be more accessible, since the space there is wider and most buildings have ramps, but the ramps can be steep slippery too. however, if you're truly interested in studying in USC, I think you can only communicate with the university how to make your stay better. although, I'm not confident they will respond to you quickly.
English_Translation: hello, personally downtown campus where pol sci is located is not accessible.. most of the entrances have stairs, and they can be steep too. doors are knobs and not all restrooms are large enough for a wheelchair. tc can be more accessible, since the space there is wider and most buildings have ramps, but the ramps can be steep slippery too. however, if you're truly interested in studying in usc, i think you can only communicate with the university how to make your stay better. although, i'm not confident they will respond to you quickly.

Data: shiftee to bs econ here unfortunately, our program is dissolved and i hope they would lift it next school year. to give a small glimpse of econ. there's a lot to learn, from the basic ideas to complex theories that would surely stir your curiosity. aside from learning the theories, graphs are your greatest friend in explaining how the theories work tho not all theories need graphs. we eat and crunch numbers here; we learn algebra, calculus, statistics, and econometrics. our profs are known to give essay questions in our major exams. you're lucky if they give mcq. one fun fact about econ in usc is that we have one course called institutional economics, which, according to our prof, is only offered here at usc. this is also probably one of my favorite courses because we study the "rules of the game" that govern the economy. as for the student body, i would say we pretty much know each other. there are only us lol. and yeah, hopefully in the future, many would consider entering econ. it won't be sbe if econ is abolished!!

Data: it took me a month to learn Bisaya as a native waynon, after a month, even my classmates in usc were surprised, and new people i met were also surprised im from samar bec i can speak straight cebuano with no the secret? use it everyday, learn and adapt the words, it is as simple as word association and conversion, example, words in waray with "r" is the same words in bisaya but using "l" instead, and more informal they use "w" or omit it completely.. second thing, mimic their expressions, intonation and accents.. guess the very best approach i did was to try to communicate in bisaya and not use tagalog as middle language at all, even if it sounds funny at first, but its a good practice.. this applies to all languages as well; i can speak business japanese too learning bisaya is easy as a waraynon, since there are a lot of common and similar words..
English_Translation: it took me a month to learn bisaya as a native waynon, after a month, even my classmates in usc were surprised, and new people i met were also surprised im from samar bec i can speak straight cebuano with no the secret? use it everyday, learn and adapt the words, it is as simple as word association and conversion, example, words in waray with "r" is the same words in bisaya but using "l" instead, and more informal they use "w" or omit it completely.. second thing, mimic their expressions, intonation and accents.. guess the very best approach i did was to try to communicate in bisaya and not use tagalog as middle language at all, even if it sounds funny at first, but its a good practice.. this applies to all languages ​​as well; i can speak business japanese too learning bisaya is easy as a waraynon, since there are a lot of common and similar words..

Data: i started studying at usc without knowing how to speak visaya. but after a while, i was able to learn the language. in just one sem, i was already able to speak full sentences and understand other people speaking bisaya. people don't really discriminate if you don't know how to speak Bisaya and they would even gladly teach you if you ask. although i had this one classmate that even though i speak bisaya to, always only speaks tagalog to me since i told him i studied in luzon for a bit. i dont know if hes trying to mock me or what bc his tone sounds like he is lol.
English_Translation: i started studying at usc without knowing how to speak visaya. but after a while, i was able to learn the language. in just one sem, i was already able to speak full sentences and understand other people speaking bisaya. people don't really discriminate if you don't know how to speak bisaya and they would even gladly teach you if you ask. although i had this one classmate that even though i speak bisaya to, always only speaks tagalog to me since i told him i studied in luzon for a bit. i dont know if hes trying to mock me or what bc his tone sounds like he is lol.

Data: speaking from experience, I'm wary again and before I went to usc some wary friends of mine gave me an impression that I would be discriminated upon for speaking in tagalog. when i got into usc tho, i was speaking in english most of the time. and the Tagalog part, you will not be burned on a stake for speaking in Tagalog. I'm in my second year now, I'm fluent in visaya but not fluent yet. I'm sure you will come across more friends when class starts, through the self-introductions in the first week, and by being friendly, hitting up conversations with people at the canteen. good luck and enjoy!
English_Translation: speaking from experience, i'm wary again and before i went to usc some wary friends of mine gave me an impression that i would be discriminated upon for speaking in tagalog. when i got into usc tho, i was speaking in english most of the time. and the tagalog part, you will not be burned on a stake for speaking in tagalog. i'm in my second year now, i'm fluent in visaya but not fluent yet. i'm sure you will come across more friends when class starts, through the self-introductions in the first week, and by being friendly, hitting up conversations with people at the canteen. good luck and enjoy!

Data: before, i had struggles of getting information from past freshman students with regards of getting in to usc so i thought i should post this so that future carolinians or those who are in the process of getting in will have something to get an advice from: my course is bsba major in marketing management and i was able to pass the exam the exam contains iq, english, mathematics, and the college readiness test. there were lots of questions each subject with only a short amount of time so i would most def say not to spend too much time in just one problem. my results were all on average level and one on below average but i still passed dw too much i had the same feeling too is the exam hard? yes. but its manageable as most of them are tackled during senior high good luck future carolinians see you on campus

Data: to all usc political science students and alumni, any tips on how to survive or be an honor student in political science?
English_Translation: to all usc political science students and alumni, any tips on how to survive or be an honor student in political science?

Data: third year polsci here! here are some things that I can suggest to survive and thrive not just in polsci, but in college in general:. find a good group of friends. as a freshie, this is what I repeated to our upperclassmen when we asked for tips. your group of friends will make or break you. if you want to be an honor student, find a group that is supportive - the type that will help each other with notes, go on productive study dates, and the likes. who you surround yourself with especially during your first year is really crucial.. stay on top of your assignments. in the first year, the workload is still manageable; but as your college journey goes on, you'll have more and more to do and it will seem like you have less and less time to do it. I suggest starting an assignment at least a week before the deadline. if the prof gave you a lot of time to work on something, it's probably for a good reason, so make good use of that time.. learn to be resourceful. there are lots of tools online that will help you in your college journey. for papers and sources that you can use in your essays and analysis papers, scihub and z-lib are highly recommended. ilovepdf is a good website for anything you want to do with pdfs and word documents. your usc email account will give you premium access to school-related websites like canva and notion, so make sure to make use of those as well because it can be paid from tuition haha.. join an org! you'll find some of the best people in these orgs, and membership to these will also look good on your cv. for polsci, you can be active in the carolinian political science society. if you're interested in student journalist, we have today's carolinian. there are other orgs out there. just keep an eye out for them.. lastly, have fun. It's true what they say "all work and no play makes Jack a dull boy". college will be the toughest four years of your life. make sure to have good school-personal life balance. study hard, but also enjoy happy moments with friends and family. good luck, op, and see you at usc polsci!
English_Translation: third year polsci here! here are some things that i can suggest to survive and thrive not just in polsci, but in college in general:. find a good group of friends. as a freshie, this is what i repeated to our upperclassmen when we asked for tips. your group of friends will make or break you. if you want to be an honor student, find a group that is supportive - the type that will help each other with notes, go on productive study dates, and the likes. who you surround yourself with especially during your first year is really crucial.. stay on top of your assignments. in the first year, the workload is still manageable; but as your college journey goes on, you'll have more and more to do and it will seem like you have less and less time to do it. i suggest starting an assignment at least a week before the deadline. if the prof gave you a lot of time to work on something, it's probably for a good reason, so make good use of that time.. learn to be resourceful. there are lots of tools online that will help you in your college journey. for papers and sources that you can use in your essays and analysis papers, scihub and z-lib are highly recommended. ilovepdf is a good website for anything you want to do with pdfs and word documents. your usc email account will give you premium access to school-related websites like canva and notion, so make sure to make use of those as well because it can be paid from tuition haha.. join an org! you'll find some of the best people in these orgs, and membership to these will also look good on your cv. for polsci, you can be active in the carolinian political science society. if you're interested in student journalist, we have today's carolinian. there are other orgs out there. just keep an eye out for them.. lastly, have fun. it's true what they say "all work and no play makes jack a dull boy". college will be the toughest four years of your life. make sure to have good school-personal life balance. study hard, but also enjoy happy moments with friends and family. good luck, op, and see you at usc polsci!

Data: i graduated high school now and i'm planning to take architecture in usc since i've always been interested but i'm still not sure if it's the right course for me. I've been hearing a lot about sleepless nights and buying expensive materials, especially for building scale models, so I'm still quite hesitant. my second option is psychology which is what i also like and what my family prefers, but i still want to try archi but i don't want to put pressure on my family for having to buy expensive art supplies. I heard it's a high demanding course too. i applied for scholarships dost and aboitiz and i'm planning to apply for ched and cebuana scholarship. i have one friend who is studying archi at ust told me that it's necessary to have a really good laptop preferably like a gaming laptop, which i don't have. lots of my family members are telling me only rich people can pursue arki and survive and we are definitely not rich so this is making me have second thoughts on the course. so i just want to ask is it really necessary to have expensive materials? do you really have to be skilled in math and science as well as arts and crafts? I'm pretty good at drawing, but I doubt that will be enough and I'm still kind of insecure about my other skills. what are all the things i need to know before i consider taking architecture as my major?
English_Translation: i graduated high school now and i'm planning to take architecture in usc since i've always been interested but i'm still not sure if it's the right course for me. i've been hearing a lot about sleepless nights and buying expensive materials, especially for building scale models, so i'm still quite hesitant. my second option is psychology which is what i also like and what my family prefers, but i still want to try archi but i don't want to put pressure on my family for having to buy expensive art supplies. i heard it's a high demanding course too. i applied for scholarships dost and aboitiz and i'm planning to apply for ched and cebuana scholarship. i have one friend who is studying archi at ust told me that it's necessary to have a really good laptop preferably like a gaming laptop, which i don't have. lots of my family members are telling me only rich people can pursue arki and survive and we are definitely not rich so this is making me have second thoughts on the course. so i just want to ask is it really necessary to have expensive materials? do you really have to be skilled in math and science as well as arts and crafts? i'm pretty good at drawing, but i doubt that will be enough and i'm still kind of insecure about my other skills. what are all the things i need to know before i consider taking architecture as my major?

Data: yes, archi is expensive. aside from the laptop, for the first few years you will be buying supplies like tech pens at least points,.,. and. each will cost around my time, not sure now. if you can maintain those pens until you graduate, then that would be good, but those pens are really easy to bend so chances are you might buy a replacement set at least once. a quality drafting set mechanical pencil, triangles, protractor, scale, t-square. you can make do with colored pencils, but colored markers kurecolor will be very helpful for making quality renders. then you have those stuff you mentioned like materials for scaled models, but those aren't as expensive. math is not hard, but we have different preferences. first few years will be basic math algebra, calc, trig then the later years will have strength of materials and more practical math. being good at drawing isn't really necessary since most of the drawing you will be doing will be drafting. so having a good drafting table at home will be a plus, but not needed since you can just work in school. but you would need to find time in between classes to do it. on top the tuition is expensive for usc in general. not sure the current rate, but in my time, my last sem was subjects only, but my tuition was k. but the main thing you have to consider. once you graduate, you still need to undergo apprenticeship for years before you can take the board. it's paid below minimum wage, some firms might pay bigger, but it's still very low. so you might have friends from other courses already having jobs that pay k-k once they graduate but you will have to work for years earning around k-k a month. just a thing to consider.
English_Translation: yes, archi is expensive. aside from the laptop, for the first few years you will be buying supplies like tech pens at least points,.,. and. each will cost around my time, not sure now. if you can maintain those pens until you graduate, then that would be good, but those pens are really easy to bend so chances are you might buy a replacement set at least once. a quality drafting set mechanical pencil, triangles, protractor, scale, t-square. you can make do with colored pencils, but colored markers kurecolor will be very helpful for making quality renders. then you have those stuff you mentioned like materials for scaled models, but those aren't as expensive. math is not hard, but we have different preferences. first few years will be basic math algebra, calc, trig then the later years will have strength of materials and more practical math. being good at drawing isn't really necessary since most of the drawing you will be doing will be drafting. so having a good drafting table at home will be a plus, but not needed since you can just work in school. but you would need to find time in between classes to do it. on top the tuition is expensive for usc in general. not sure the current rate, but in my time, my last sem was subjects only, but my tuition was k. but the main thing you have to consider. once you graduate, you still need to undergo apprenticeship for years before you can take the board. it's paid below minimum wage, some firms might pay bigger, but it's still very low. so you might have friends from other courses already having jobs that pay k-k once they graduate but you will have to work for years earning around k-k a month. just a thing to consider.

Data: hello, may i ask what are the st year subjects in psychology in usc? and what are the ones i should focus on the most?
English_Translation: hello, may i ask what are the st year subjects in psychology in usc? and what are the ones i should focus on the most?

Data: most subjects during first year are general education only. major subject for st sem is general psychology, while nd sem includes psychological statistics, physiological psychology, and positive psychology if there are no changes in the syllabus during our time in. focus on the basics of psych such as statistics during your first year, this will build your foundation for psychological assessment. in general, board exam subjects are important since they have departmentals: developmental psychology, industrial organizational psych, abnormal psychology, and most importantly, psychological assessment. also, even though theories of personality isn't a board exam subject anymore, it is still good to have a solid foundation on this since this is a subject that revolves around psychology. i hope you'd consider usc psych! it's fun, promise, you'll eventually enjoy it.
English_Translation: most subjects during first year are general education only. major subject for st sem is general psychology, while nd sem includes psychological statistics, physiological psychology, and positive psychology if there are no changes in the syllabus during our time in. focus on the basics of psych such as statistics during your first year, this will build your foundation for psychological assessment. in general, board exam subjects are important since they have departmentals: developmental psychology, industrial organizational psych, abnormal psychology, and most importantly, psychological assessment. also, even though theories of personality isn't a board exam subject anymore, it is still good to have a solid foundation on this since this is a subject that revolves around psychology. i hope you'd consider usc psych! it's fun, promise, you'll eventually enjoy it.

Data: tips as a g new student who had noooo idea what usc was like!! - must try your best and study hard in subjects that you are weak in, not to pressure but i think you will thank yourself in the future hihi like me who struggles chem - phones are allowed but must be kept during class unless needed some rba teachers ky strict ani - for first week of classes, i recommend not buying things yet.. maybe js a notebook and penspencils? the teachers will tell u what to buy and the requirements in their class but for me, starting nd sem i used tabletipad nlng but depends on the teacher if you allow or no notebooks sa requirement hihi - buy a clearbooooook, some teachers will collect papers and i swear the most useful jd ni buy yellow paper as well vv useful - be aware that long quizzes have hugeee impact on your grades, teachers like in bascalstatprecal consider long quizzes as performance tasks already so try ur best - create friends and connections, this will def help you feel less alone and allows you to ask for help easier! also don't be afraid to ask for help! - some subjects hv a lot of activities such as public speaking, presentations, and recitations for me, st sem was the hardest, costs mga ways of activities, get out of the comfort zone huhu - chemlab teachers are more stricter than regular teachers so do your workjournals in time and prelab exercises as well! chemlab have different periodic exams than other subjs, its not entirely multiple choice.. more on moving examssolving but its all based on your modules and lab experiments but honestly, as someone who was not used to the school nd the area, js have fun with classmates and laag around the city thats what i did most of the time but yk have balance! good luck!!:
